toolchain.note: Upstream publishes ONNX directly; no paddle2onnx step. Shapes fixed with onnxslim 0.1.59.

dictionary.layout: line 0 = 'blank' placeholder, then characters, then a trailing space
dictionary.warning: medium and small share this charset; tiny is a separate 6906-class charset and must use its own file

task_metric.name: greedy CTC decode matches PaddleOCR output on the same crop
task_metric.measured_onnx: PASS at 48x960; at 48x320 a 19.8:1 crop loses characters to the aspect squeeze (expected, not a defect)

measured.note: Both models compiled to .hbm on OE 3.7.0 (HBRT 4.7.5). All int8 PTQ. Not yet board-run, so no latency or end-to-end accuracy.

det.gelu_placement: all 28 GELU nodes ON=BPU (no CPU fallback)

rec.calibration: 64 real line crops, cut from the 24 det pages by the det ONNX

measured.assessment: Both output cosines (0.98 det, 0.97 rec) sit below the 0.99 layer-A gate. This is int8 on transformer-ish OCR heads and is expected; DB detection thresholds its probability map and CTC recognition is argmax-per-step, so both tolerate some fidelity loss. Whether that costs real recall / character accuracy is a BOARD question -- compare against an int16 build only if it does. Do not fail these on cosine alone (see CONVERSION.md). BOARD RESULT: det layer B bit-identical; rec layer B task-equivalent (2e-4 logit drift, identical decode). Layer C confirms real int8 character loss on a hard crop -- an int16 rec build is the next experiment if accuracy matters.

open_questions: RESOLVED for detection: all 28 GELU (Erf/HzGelu) nodes show ON=BPU in node_info, i.e. no CPU fallback. ON=BPU is trustworthy (unlike ON=--). Latency still unmeasured until board.
Recognition (SVTR stack) not yet compiled. If int8 output cosine is poor, read per-layer cosine only to localise, then consider int16 -- see CONVERSION.md.
Detection output cosine 0.98 (int8): compare against an int16 det build if box recall degrades on the board.

layer_C_hbm_vs_onnx.interpretation: Layer B is bit-identical, so this is pure int8 quantisation loss, not a port defect. DB detection thresholds its probability map; whether 0.98 costs box recall needs a boxed comparison, not a cosine.

layer_B_hbm_vs_bc.interpretation: board and host decode to identical text, but the logits differ by 2e-4. This model is mixed int8/int16/int32 with float CPU ops; board and host float implementations differ in the last places. Bit-identity holds for pure fixed-point graphs (las2, det), not necessarily for mixed ones. The right layer-B check here is task equivalence.

layer_C_hbm_vs_onnx.interpretation: Real int8 loss: board int8 decode drops several characters the float ONNX keeps. On this deliberately hard 48x320 crop (a 19.8:1 line squeezed to 6.7:1) int8 is visibly worse. This is the concrete answer to whether int8 rec is good enough: on hard inputs, not without an int16 comparison.

int16_recognition.why: int8 recognition drops characters on hard inputs; this quantifies whether int16 is worth it.
int16_recognition.method: 20 aspect-fitting real line crops (<=6.7:1, so float decodes cleanly and the aspect limit is out of the picture). Compare each build's board decode to the float ONNX decode.

layer_B.int8: cosine 1.0 but 2e-4 drift (mixed precision has float CPU ops)

int16_recognition.recommendation: Use the int16 build (config_rec_int16.yaml) when recognition accuracy matters. int8 is fine for easy, well-cropped text; on harder crops it visibly degrades. Latency cost of int16 is unmeasured on the board.
